segments.s326-g89: که here begins a subordinate content clause meaning 'that'. ما هم برآنیم is an idiom: ما (we) + هم (also) + برآنیم (we are of that mind / we agree), present stative first-person plural. کاین is an archaic contraction of که این ('that this' / 'that which') introducing what follows, and پیر گفت literally 'the elder said' (پیر = 'elder/old man' here); together کاین پیر گفت is the clause reported as the thing they agree with.
segments.s326-g90: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning 'one must not / should not'. در راستی is a nominal phrase ('in truth' / 'the matter of truth') functioning as the object; the following را is the definite/object marker attaching to that object. نهفت is an archaic verbal form (perfect/subjunctive-form of نهفتن) meaning 'be hidden' / 'hide', so the whole clause means 'one should not hide the truth.'
segments.s326-g81: گر introduces a real conditional clause ('if'). این پند من یک به یک is an adverbial/NP: این پند من = 'this advice of mine' (possessive expressed by noun + من), and یک به یک is an adverbial phrase 'one by one'. نشنوی is the 2nd-person singular present (negative formed with ن- + شنوی) meaning 'you do not hear/heed' — here used in the protasis ('if' clause) describing failure to follow advice.
segments.s326-g82: بگروی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive form (classical prefix ب- + verb) meaning 'you will take/join/adhere'. The phrase is ordered as indirect object/place marker + complement: به آهرمن ('to/with Ahriman') + بدکنش ('the ill‑doer') — i.e. you will join/attach the role of evildoer to Ahriman or take Ahriman's side. The construction expresses the warned consequence of the protasis.
segments.s326-g83: بماندت and نماندت are jussive/optative or predictive classical forms (prefix ب- with verbal stem) meaning '(will) remain' and 'will not remain'. Here they express expected consequence: 'pain will remain' (بماندت درد) and 'fortune will not remain' (نماندت بخت). چوordinate وَ links the two clauses. The personal reference is generic/impersonal (it/you will be left).
segments.s326-g84: نه ... نه ... نه is the standard Persian negative coordination 'neither ... nor ... nor ...'. اورنگ شاهی is a compound noun meaning 'royal seat/throne' (اورنگ + شاهی), and تاج و تخت are synonymous items 'crown and throne'. The verbs are omitted here (elliptical) because the previous clause supplies the predicate ('will not remain').
segments.s326-g85: خرد باد is an optative formula: خرد (subject) + باد (optative/may it be) = 'may wisdom be...'. جان ترا is 'your life/soul' expressed with the 2nd-person clitic ت plus the object marker را (here marking the definite/affected noun). رهنمای is predicate noun 'guide'. The whole phrase functions as a blessing: 'May wisdom be the guide of your life.'
segments.s326-g86: بماناد is again the jussive/optative 3rd-person form 'may ... remain'. The adverbial به پاکی 'in/with purity' qualifies what should remain. مغزت is 'your mind/brain' (morphology: noun + 2nd-person suffix), and به جای 'in its place' is an idiom meaning 'intact/where it belongs'. Together: 'may your mind remain pure and in its place.'
segments.s326-g87: چو آمد = 'when (it) came' (چو = when; آمد = came). سخنهای دستان is a genitival noun phrase 'the words/speeches of the dāstān(s)/tales' (سخنهای = the words/speeches; دستان here = 'tales/stories'). به بن = 'to the root/base' (an idiom meaning to the point or fundamental moment). The clause sets a temporal point: 'When the tales' words reached the root/turning point...'.
segments.s326-g88: یلان برگشادند is a simple past plural: یلان 'the heroes' (subject) + برگشادند 'they opened/revealed/spoke' (they began/sallied forth). یکسر here modifies the following noun or the verb phrase and means 'all at once/altogether'; سخن 'speech' names what they opened — i.e. they all began speaking together. The verb precedes the adverbial modifier in this poetic order but the meaning is 'the heroes spoke all at once.'
segments.s326-g33: ز یک‌سو is a prepositional/adverbial phrase ('from/on one side'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from/on' and یک‌سو ('one side') is the adverbial. نبیره (scion/offspring) is the head noun and it is immediately followed by افراسیاب (the name) without an explicit ezafe — a common poetic compression where the genitive/attributive relation is implied: 'a scion of Afrasiab.'
segments.s326-g34: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding noun ('who/which'). جز ... را is the restrictor meaning 'except ...' with را marking the definite object. ندیدی is a negative past form 'did not see' (classical/poetic past) agreeing with the relative subject; به خواب is a locative phrase 'in (his) sleep / in a dream.' Word order is typical classical Persian: relative clause after the noun it modifies.
segments.s326-g35: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' کاووس is the subject and دژخیم is an epithet/appositive ('cruel, harsh') placed after the name in the usual epic style. دیگر + نیا forms the predicate; this is elliptical/archaic wording where 'دیگر' = 'another/any more' and نیا here functions as a verbal/predicative element (the exact classical sense is archaic or compressed), so the phrase means roughly 'no other [came/was born].'
segments.s326-g36: پر از ... is a repeated adjective phrase 'full of ...' (پر 'full' + از 'of'). رنگ رخ is a genitive compound 'the color/complexion of the face' and دل پر از کیمیا parallels it: 'the heart full of kimia.' There is no copula; these are descriptive nominal phrases that complete the characterization.
segments.s326-g37: This gives the extent: ز خاور ... تا باختر 'from the East ... as far as the West.' ورا is a deictic/adverbial particle ('there/thither') inserted for emphasis, and بود is the past copula 'was' linking the locative/extent phrase to what follows. The sequence is slightly inverted for poetic effect but means his domain/renown stretched from east to west.
segments.s326-g38: A simple nominal list (connected by و) giving the things possessed or noted: بزرگی (greatness), شاهی (kingship/sovereignty), تاج (crown), کمر (girdle/belt). The nouns are in apposition to the preceding clause and there is no repeated verb — the copula from the previous segment covers them.
segments.s326-g39: همی is an adverb indicating continuous or habitual action ('continually/always'); خواست is the past tense 'he wished/wanted.' کز is the classical fused form of که + ز meaning 'that/from which' and here introduces the content clause. بگذرد is the present/subjunctive (3rd sg.) form of 'to pass' used as the verb of the content clause: 'that he might pass/become beyond the sky.'
segments.s326-g40: همه گردش اختران is the direct object of the wished-for action and means 'the entire circuit/revolution of the stars.' بشمرد is the present/subjunctive 3rd-person form 'he might count' (subsequent jussive sense after خواست), so the parallel infinitival/subjunctive clauses express two wishes: to pass the sky and to count the stars' revolutions.
segments.s326-g73: پشیمانی آید ترا ز این سخن is a verbless-order inversion common in poetry: the noun پشیمانی (regret) is placed first and the verb آید (comes) follows, with ترا (to you) as the indirect object. ز here (poetic ز) marks source/reason (“from/because of”), so the whole clause means “regret will come to you from these words,” i.e. “you will regret these words.”
segments.s326-g74: براندیش is an imperative (or hortative) meaning “consider/think it over” (literally “turn your thought”). The conjunction و links the command to the following negative imperative فرمان دیوان مکن, where فرمان (order/decree) is the object and دیوان names the royal office; مکن is the negative imperative “do not (make/do).”
segments.s326-g75: وگر (if) begins a conditional clause; نیز here has the sense “also/still” (i.e. even if you continue). جویی is the second-person present/subjunctive form of جستن/جوییدن (“you seek/pursue”) used in conditional sense; چنین کار means “such a deed” (چنین = “such,” کار = “deed/affair”), and دیو is the agent “demon.”
segments.s326-g76: ببرد is third-person present/future sense of بردن (“takes/carries away”); ز (poetic for از) marks source/possession “from you”; تو is the pronoun “you.” فر here is the technical noun for royal glory/splendour, کیهان qualifies it as “of the world,” and خدیو is a vocative address (“O Khedive”/ruler). The clause thus says the demon will strip you of your worldly royal glory.
segments.s326-g77: بمانی is second-person singular present/future of ماندن (“you will remain/you will be left”), followed by two adjectival phrases formed with پر از (“full of”): پر از درد = “full of pain” (درد here emphasizes emotional sorrow) and دل پر گناه = “a heart full of sin.” The two halves are coordinated by و.
segments.s326-g78: نخوانند is a third-person plural negative form meaning “they will not call/read/name.” In this context خواند is used in the sense “call/name,” so نخوانند ... ترا شاه means “they will not call you king.” از این پس is an adverbial phrase “henceforth / from now on”; ترا is the object pronoun (t+ra) and نیز here functions as an emphatic/also-like particle in the negative statement.
segments.s326-g79: به یزدان پناه is an exhortation “take refuge in God” (به + noun + پناه). The second half به یزدان گرای repeats the prepositional heading and uses گرای as an imperative “turn/lean toward” (i.e. “be inclined to God / resort to God”). The coordination with و links two parallel imperatives.
segments.s326-g80: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause “for/because,” and اویست here functions syntactically as ‘he is’ (classical copular form), so the clause states a quality of God. بر نیک و بد رهنمای means “a guide over good and bad” (بر = over/toward, نیک = good, بد = bad, رهنمای = guide); together: “for He is the guide regarding good and evil.” Note: اویست in the lemma list appears as a proper name but here is the copula/compound form “he is,” so that listed name senses do not apply.
segments.s326-g1: غمی شد is a verbal phrase meaning "a grief occurred/befell" (شد used in the sense of "happen/come about"). دل ایرانیان را is the affected experiencer phrase (دل = "heart"; ایرانیان = "the Iranians") marked with را to topicalize/mark the person whose heart is affected. ز شاه is a causal prepositional phrase "because of the king." The order is poetic: event (غمی شد) + affected party (دل ایرانیان را) + cause (ز شاه).
segments.s326-g2: همه خیره گشتند is a past-tense plural clause: همه "everyone" + خیره "astonished/staring" + گشتند (گشتن used here as "became/turned (into a state)") — thus "all became astonished." The conjunction و links a following participial phrase: گم‌کرده راه is a compound of the participle گم‌کرده "having lost" modifying راه "way," i.e. an elliptical predicate meaning "and (were) lost of way/astray."
segments.s326-g3: چو بشنید is a temporal clause: چو "when" + بشنید (past of شنیدن) "he heard." زال is the subject; این سخن is the object/phrase he heard. بردمید is an archaic/poetic past verb meaning to lift/throw oneself up or to seize (here best rendered as "sprang up"/"lifted himself"). The sequence is temporal: when X heard Y, (then) he did Z.
segments.s326-g4: یکی باد سرد functions as a nominal phrase introduced by یکی "a/certain," followed by باد "wind" and the adjective سرد "cold": "a cold wind." از جگر is a metaphorical source phrase "from the jigar (literally "liver," here the seat of feeling/heart)." برکشید is a past transitive verb "he drew up/raised" (برکشیدن = to lift/draw up), so the whole clause reads literally "he drew up a cold wind from his heart/breast," a poetic way to describe a cold sigh or a shudder.
segments.s326-g5: به ایرانیان گفت is a speech formula: به + indirect object (ایرانیان) "to the Iranians" + گفت (past of گفتن) "he said." کاین (که + این contracted) introduces reported content: کاین رای نیست = "that this counsel/opinion is not (proper)." Note that کاین introduces the subordinate clause that reports the content of گفت.
segments.s326-g6: خرد را is topicalized by را (here marking the theme "wisdom" as the thing being spoken about). به مغز اندرش is a locative phrase: به مغز = "into/into the brain," اندرش = اندر + ش "in his" (i.e. "in his brain"). جای نیست is a negative existential phrase "there is no place/room." Together: "there is no place for wisdom in his brain."
segments.s326-g7: که تا introduces a purposive/temporal clause: که (here = "so/that") + تا ("so that/in order that"). من is the first‑person subject. ببستم is an archaic/poetic past/subjunctive form of بستن here used in the sense "gird (one's loins)" (بستن کمر = to gird one’s waist/prepare oneself). کمر is the object ("waist/girdle/loins"), and بر میان is a locative phrase meaning "into the midst/among" (بر + میان = toward/in the middle).
segments.s326-g8: پرستنده‌ام is a predicate formed from پرستنده + ـام; here it functions as "I am a worshipper/devotee" (the clitic ـام marking first person). پیش تخت کیان is a locative/relational phrase: پیش = "before/in the presence of," تخت = "throne," کیان = "the Kians/kings" (genitive relation). The whole phrase places the speaker among worshippers before the Kian throne.
segments.s326-g41: بدان is the classical/poetic compound imperative 'ب' + 'دان' meaning 'know (that)'; here it introduces what follows. بر is a preposition meaning 'about/on/regarding'. پندها is the object 'admonitions' placed before the verb (typical Persian word order), and دادمش is past tense 'I gave' with the enclitic -ش ('to him'), so the clause means 'Know that I gave him many admonitions.'
segments.s326-g42: همین تلخ گفتار is a noun phrase 'this same bitter speech/words' (همین = 'this same'; تلخ used figuratively = 'harsh/bitter'); بگشادمش is the past of بگشودن 'to open/reveal' with the enclitic -ش ('to him'), so the verb means 'I revealed/opened it to him.'
segments.s326-g43: بسی پند is 'many/much advice' placed before the verb; بشنید is third person past 'he heard'. و links the clauses. سودی نکرد literally 'did no profit' (سودی = 'benefit/profit'), here meaning 'it was of no use' or 'he gained no advantage.'
segments.s326-g44: از او is the prepositional phrase 'from him'; بازگشتم is first person past 'I returned' (باز- + برگشتن). پر از introduces a description 'full of ...' and the nouns داغ و درد follow: داغ here is figurative 'burning grief' and درد is 'sorrow/pain', so the clause means 'I returned from him full of burning grief and sorrow.'
segments.s326-g45: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بر شد نگون is a verbal phrase where شد = 'became/was' and نگون = 'overthrown/turned down' (predicate); اندر آمد به خاک literally 'came into the earth' = 'fell to the ground', with اندر آمد as past 'he came/fell' and به خاک marking the destination.
segments.s326-g46: ببخشود is a form of بخشودن 'to forgive/grant' (here functioning as an optative/wish or simple past 'forgave' depending on scope). بر جانش = 'for/on his life' (بر used prepositionally) with جانش = 'his life' (جان + enclitic -ش). یزدان پاک (the pure God) follows the verb as subject in inverted order: 'May the pure God forgive his life.'
segments.s326-g47: بیامد is third person past 'he came'. به یزدان = 'to/ before God'. شده ناسپاس is a participial clause 'having become ungrateful' (شده = 'become/being', ناسپاس = 'ungrateful'), describing his state on coming.
segments.s326-g48: This is a descriptive pair: سری پر ز گرد = 'a head full of dust' (سری = 'head', پر = 'full', ز گرد = 'of dust' with poetic ز = از), و دلی پرهراس = 'and a heart full of dread' (دلی here describing the heart, پرهراس = 'full of fear/terrified'). Together they modify his appearance/mood.
segments.s326-g57: This is a noun phrase listing the victims. Persian lists nouns with و between them (زن و کودک) and places the adjective after the noun: کودک خرد = 'small/young child'. ایرانیان at the end functions as the possessor/agent group 'of the Iranians' (the whole phrase = 'the women and small children of the Iranians').
segments.s326-g58: ببردی is a 2nd-person past (archaic/poetic) form meaning 'you carried off / you took away' with the victims understood from the previous phrase. به کین is a prepositional phrase 'out of vengeance'. کس نبستی میان literally 'you did not (leave/tie) anyone between' — here نبستی (negative past of بستن) + میان 'between' expresses that you left no one among/remaining; the object 'کس' is indefinite/negative ('no one') because of the negated verb.
segments.s326-g59: ترا here is the object 'you' (written in the line as ت + را); the sequence 'ترا ایزد' places the object before the subject for emphasis. از دست او رسته is a participial phrase: رسته (past participle of رستن) = 'freed/released', with از دست او = 'from his hand' (دست = power/authority). کرد is the light verb completing the verbal phrase: 'God caused/made (you) freed' → 'God freed you.'
segments.s326-g60: ببخشود is a past (preterite) verb 'he forgave / pardoned'. The conjunction و links the next clause. رای تو پیوسته کرد is a causative/result clause: رای 'your counsel/plan' (رای = counsel/plan), پیوسته (adverb/adjective 'continuous/firm') and کرد 'he made/did' — together 'he established/made your counsel continuous' = 'he confirmed/ensured your plan.'
segments.s326-g61: بکشتی here is an archaic second-person past form meaning 'you killed' (the other possible sense 'by ship' is irrelevant in context). کسی را is the direct object (را marks definiteness/emphasis). که introduces a relative clause; ز او بد هراس = 'in great fear of him' where ز ('from/of') marks the source of the fear, بد intensifies هراس ('great fear/terror').
segments.s326-g62: This is a descriptive continuation applying to that person: به دادار دارنده = 'toward the Creator, the Holder/Sustainer' with به marking the target/recipient of the following characterization, and بد ناسپاس = 'evil/ungrateful' (ناسپاس = ungrateful). In Persian the adjective ناسپاس follows the noun(s) it qualifies.
segments.s326-g63: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. گفتم is 'I said' (the listed senses for گفتم in the metadata do not include the speech sense, so its sense index is reported as -1). که introduces the content clause 'that...'. هنگام آرام بود is a normal predication 'the time was calm' with هنگام 'at the time', آرام 'calm', and بود 'was' as the past copula/auxiliary.
segments.s326-g64: گه here means 'occasion / sometimes' (temporal/dispositional noun). The three nouns بخشش و پوشش و جام are coordinated by و and are predicated by بود at the end: 'there were forgiveness (pardons), shelter/protection, and a cup' — a listing of comforts/mercies available at that time. بود is the past copula 'was/were'.
segments.s326-g9: ز شاهان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'among/from the kings' (ز used poetically). ندیدم Kisi is verb + indefinite object: ندیدم ('I did not see') + کسی ('anyone'), and the following کاین بگفت is a relative clause ('who said this') where کاین is the demonstrative 'this/that' and بگفت is the past-tense narrative 'said' modifying کسی.
segments.s326-g10: چو او گفت is a temporal clause 'when/as he spoke' (چو = 'when'); گفت here is the verb 'said' in the past narrative. ما را is an affected-party construction (را marks the experiencer/object 'to us'), and نباید نهفت is an impersonal modal construction: نباید ('ought not / must not') + نهفت ('to be hidden/hidden') yields 'it should not be hidden from us.'
segments.s326-g11: نباید is the impersonal modal 'one should not / ought not'. بدین is the prepositional phrase 'in/with this' (ب- + this), and بود همداستان is the copular structure 'to be of the same mind' (بود = 'to be', همداستان = 'like-minded'). Together: 'one ought not to be like-minded about this.'
segments.s326-g12: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that'); او is the subject 'he', هیچ here functions adverbially as 'ever/at all', and راند is the past-tense verb 'drove/expelled'. چنین داستان is the object phrase 'such a story/tale'. The whole clause gives the content they should not agree with: 'that he ever drove such a tale.'
segments.s326-g13: مگر introduces an exception ('except that / unless'). دیو is the subject 'a demon'; با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him', and هم‌آواز گشت is a predicative phrase: هم‌آواز ('in unison/agreeing') + گشت (past of گشتن 'became/turned'), so 'a demon became aligned with him.'
segments.s326-g14: که introduces a result/explanatory clause ('so that/that'); از راه یزدان is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by the way/path of the divine/God' (از = 'by/from', راه = 'way', یزدان = 'the divine/God'), and سرش بازگشت is a verbal clause 'his head returned' (سر = 'head', ش = 'his', بازگشت = 'returned' in the past), i.e. 'his head was turned back' or 'he was restored/turned.'
segments.s326-g15: A simple nominal phrase listing two proper names 'فریدون و هوشنگ' joined by و ('and'), followed by یزدان‌پرست as an appositive/adjective meaning 'God-worshipper' specifying their religious character.
segments.s326-g16: نبردند here functions as past tense 'they took/did not take' (in context with هرگز it gives 'they never took'); هرگز = 'never', and بدین کار دست is an idiomatic phrase 'hand to this work' (ب- + این = 'to/at this', کار = 'task', دست = 'hand') meaning 'to set hand to this task' or 'to undertake this work.' Together: 'they never undertook this deed.'
segments.s326-g49: تو رفتی is a simple past, 2nd-person singular (‘you went’). و links the clauses. شمشیرزن صد هزار is a poetic noun + numeral order meaning ‘a hundred thousand swordsmen’ (the numeral follows the noun here for metrical/poetic reasons rather than the more typical modern order).
segments.s326-g50: زره‌دار is a postnominal descriptive noun ‘armored (ones)/armored warriors’; با introduces the instrument or accompaniment ‘with’; گرزه is the weapon ‘mace’; گاوسار here is given in apposition to گرزه and functions as the name of the mace (or a proper name attached to it), so the whole phrase reads ‘armored (men) with the mace Gâwsar.’
segments.s326-g51: چو here introduces a simile ‘like/as’; شیر ژیان is an epithet ‘living/lively lion’ (شیر + ژیان) serving as the image of the actor; ساختی is past tense, 2nd-person singular ‘you made/formed’; رزم را (را marks the definite direct object) receives the action: ‘you made/formed the battle (like a living lion).’ Word order is shaped for poetic rhythm: the simile phrase precedes the verb and its object.
segments.s326-g52: بیاراستی is a verbal past-form here meaning ‘you arrayed/you adorned/you set in order’ (an archaic or poetic form of آراستن); دشت خوارزم را is the definite object phrase ‘the plain of Khwarezm’ (دشت = plain, خوارزم = place-name) with را marking it as definite object. The verb precedes its object for metrical reasons.
segments.s326-g53: ز پیش سپه is a prepositional phrase ‘from the front of the army/host’ (ز = poetic from, پیش = front, سپه = army). تیز is an adverb ‘swiftly’ qualifying رفتی, which is the past 2nd-person ‘you went’; به جنگ marks direction ‘into/to battle.’ Adverbial and prepositional elements are ordered for poetic rhythm.
segments.s326-g54: پیاده شدی is a light-verb construction meaning ‘you became (or were) on foot’ — i.e. you dismounted or fought as infantry (شدی = became). پس is a sequential adverb ‘then’; به جنگ پشنگ = ‘to the battle with/against Pashang’ where پشنگ is a proper name (the opponent).
segments.s326-g55: گر introduces a conditional ‘if’. او را is the object ‘him’ (را marks definiteness). بدی بر تو means ‘he did harm against you’ (بدی = harm, بر = against/upon, تو = you). بر دست‌یاب is an idiomatic verbal phrase ‘lay hands on / seize’ where دست‌یاب (literally ‘attain by hand’) is used in the potential/optative sense with بر prefixed; in classical Persian dependent forms like this express the potential/result of the condition (‘you would be able to seize him’).
segments.s326-g56: به ایران is the directional phrase ‘into Iran’; کشیدی is past tense 2nd-person ‘you drew/you drove’ (here ‘you drove back’); رد افراسیاب is the object phrase ‘the retreat/flight of Afrasiab’ (رد used as ‘passage/retreat’). The verb governs the noun phrase and the prepositional phrase gives the destination.
segments.s326-g25: ز + NP marks the source: 'ز پیر جهاندیده' = 'from the elder who has seen the world.' بشنو is an imperative 'listen/hear' addressed to the addressee, and سخن is its direct object. Adjective جهاندیده follows the noun پیر as is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s326-g26: چو introduces a conditional ('if'); کژ is an adjective modifying رای. آورد is a simple past/present literary form 'brings/presents' (here 'brings forward'), رای = 'counsel/opinion', and پاسخ مکن is the negative imperative ('do not answer'), with مکن giving the prohibition.
segments.s326-g27: که functions as a causal 'for/because' linking this remark to the previous line. گفتار is the subject 'speech/utterance' and تلخست is an older 3rd‑person copula form meaning 'is bitter.' با + راستی indicates the instrument or circumstance 'with truth' (i.e. when it is true).
segments.s326-g28: ببندد is a literary 3rd‑person (optative/imperative‑like) verb 'close/end' (here: 'one should end it'). به تلخی is an adverbial phrase 'with bitterness' (به marking manner), در کاستی is another adverbial phrase 'in brevity/shortness'—در + kaasti expresses the measure or limitation in which the speech should be closed.
segments.s326-g29: نشاید is an impersonal negative 'it is not fitting/appropriate.' که here introduces the subordinate clause 'that you…'. آزار گیری is a verbal phrase 'take offense / take harm' (آزار = annoyance, گیری = 'taking'); ز من = 'from me' (prepositional object).
segments.s326-g30: بر این راستی is a topical/covering phrase 'concerning this truth' (بر marks respect/relation to the noun), and پیش این انجمن means 'in front of / before this assembly' (پیش = 'in the presence of'). Together they locate the reason/time/place for which taking offense would be inappropriate.
segments.s326-g31: به توران زمین places the action in/into the land of Turan ('به' here indicating direction/place). زادی 'of noble birth / descendant' + از مادرت ('from your mother' — از = 'from', ت = 2nd‑person possessive suffix on مادر) indicates lineage: 'a scion/offspring from your mother.'
segments.s326-g32: همانجا 'there' refers back to Turan; بد آرام is a compound descriptive phrase 'ill at ease / badly settled' (بد = 'ill/poorly', آرام = 'at rest/calm'). و links the next term; آبشخورت is a poetic form meaning 'your water‑source / source of sustenance' (آبشخور as a figurative support), here describing that person or his condition.
segments.s326-g65: به ایران is a prepositional place phrase (classical usage: 'to/into/within Iran') placed before the rest; کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. کار is the nominal subject ('affair/task'), and دشوارتر is a comparative adjective ('more difficult') serving as the predicative. The adjective uses the comparative suffix -تر and the clause omits an explicit copula (typical of Persian).
segments.s326-g66: This short clause strings comparative descriptions without an explicit verb (elliptical predicate). فزون‌تر is a comparative/augmentative ('more abundant/greater'), followed by بدی ('evil') as the noun being increased; دل پرآزار‌تر is another coordinated predicative phrase where دل ('heart') takes the compound comparative adjective پرآزار‌تر ('more full of torment' / 'more troubled').
segments.s326-g67: که here introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'). تو is the 2nd-person subject. برنوشتی is a past (perfect/preterite) 2nd‑person singular of the poetic verb برنوشتن ('to write/inscribe'); in context it functions as a perfective action applied to the following object ره ایزدی ('the divine way'), with the adjective ایزدی following the noun (classical order).
segments.s326-g68: به کژی is a prepositional phrase 'into/onto crookedness'; گذشتی is the past 2nd‑person singular of گذشتن ('you passed/went'), connecting the action to that state. و is the coordinating 'and', and راه بدی is a noun phrase 'an evil/bad path' (راه = 'path', بدی = 'evil'). The sequence describes successive actions of the subject.
segments.s326-g69: از این بد is an ablative phrase 'from this evil'. نباشد is a negative present/subjunctive/optative form (impersonal here: 'will not be' / 'is not to be'), followed by تنت ('your body' — تن with the 2nd‑person suffix) and سودمند as the predicate adjective 'beneficial/useful'. Word order is somewhat inverted: the phrase means roughly 'this evil will not make your body beneficial' / 'your body will not prosper because of this evil.'
segments.s326-g70: نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive/impersonal verb form ('does not come / will not be fitting'); جهان‌آفرین is 'the world‑creator' (God/Creator) and را is the object/contrast marker attached to that noun. پسند is a noun meaning 'liking/approval' (here part of the idiom نیاید ... پسند = 'it will not please/ be to the liking of'). The whole unit means 'it will not please the Creator.'
segments.s326-g71: گر is the conditional 'if'. این باشد is a clause 'this be/hold' (باشد is the subjunctive/present of 'to be' used in a conditional). ای شاه is the vocative 'O king'. سامان تو is 'your order/state' with the possessive pronoun placed after the noun (classical emphasis/order). The line sets the condition and addresses the king directly.
segments.s326-g72: نگردد is a negative present/subjunctive/optative form of گشتن ('may not become/turn / will not happen'); کسی is 'anyone/no one' in this negative context. گرد here is the postpositional/relational 'around' (forming گرد پیمان = 'around the covenant' / 'in respect of the pact'), and پیمان تو is 'your covenant/pledge' (پیمان + your pronoun تو). Literally 'no one will turn about your covenant' — idiomatically 'no one will keep/honor your covenant.'
segments.s326-g17: بگویم is a first‑person present/subjunctive form meaning 'let me say' / 'I will say' used here to introduce what the speaker intends to tell. بدو is the prepositional object 'to him'; من is an emphatic/explicit subject 'I'; همه راستی is a noun phrase 'the whole truth' (همه 'whole/entire' + راستی 'truth'). Word order is verb-first (classical/poetic) with the object phrase following.
segments.s326-g18: گر is the conditional particle 'if' (or 'should'); آید is the present/subjunctive of آمدن 'to come' used here as 'should come'. به جان اندرون is a locative phrase 'into the inner soul/heart' (به + جان + اندرون), and کاستی is the noun 'deficiency/shortcoming' that is the thing which might come. The clause expresses a conditional: 'if a lack comes into the inner heart.'
segments.s326-g19: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' setting the manner; یافت is a past form 'was found' / 'obtained' (here passive/intransitive sense: an answer was found); پاسخ is the noun 'answer' (the thing found); ز ایرانیان indicates the source/agent 'from the Iranians' (poetic z = 'from'). The clause literally reads 'thus an answer was found from the Iranians.'
segments.s326-g20: کز is a contracted form of 'که' meaning 'that' and introduces the content of the answer. این‌سان سخن = 'speech/words of this kind'; کس نگفت is a negative indefinite construction meaning 'no one said' (کس = 'no one' here, نگفت = 'did not say' / 'had not said'); از میان = 'from among' / 'out of (them)'. Together: 'that no one among them had spoken thus.'
segments.s326-g21: همه با توایم is a compact clause 'we are all with you' — توایم is an archaic/formal first‑person form meaning 'I am with you' but here used collectively with همه ('all of us are with you'). آنچه گویی is a relative clause 'what you say/shall say' (آنچه = 'that which/what', گویی = 2nd person present/subjunctive 'you say' used to report the anticipated speech). به شاه is the prepositional complement 'to the king.'
segments.s326-g22: مبادا is an optative/negative jussive meaning 'may it not be' or 'lest'; که introduces the subordinate clause. او گم کند is a present/subjunctive phrasing 'he should lose' (گم + کند, literally 'make lost' or 'become lost'); رسم و راه are the objects 'custom and way' (two nouns joined by و). The whole is a wish: 'may he not lose (or abandon) custom and way.'
segments.s326-g23: شنید این سخن is a simple past clause 'he heard these words' (شنید = 'heard', این سخن = 'this speech/these words'). زال is the proper name 'Zāl' (the subject of the following action). برپای خاست is an idiomatic phrase 'rose to his feet' / 'stood up' (برپای = 'on [his] feet', خاست = 'arose'). The line combines hearing and the immediate physical reaction.
segments.s326-g24: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus', گفت past 'said'); کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; خسرو is the addressee 'Khosrow' (name/title). داد و راست are epithets/nouns placed after the name meaning 'justice and truth' (داد = 'justice' used as an epithet). The segment introduces Zāl's speech addressed to Khosrow.

sentences.s326-s45: We too hold, as that elder said, that one should not conceal the truth.
sentences.s326-s41: If you do not heed this advice of mine, one by one, you will join with Ahriman, the evildoer.
sentences.s326-s42: You will be left with sorrow and will not have fortune — neither royal glory, nor crown, nor throne.
sentences.s326-s43: May wisdom be the guide of your life; may your mind remain pure and in its place.
sentences.s326-s44: When the words of the tales reached the root, the heroes all began to speak at once.
sentences.s326-s17: On one side a scion of Afrasiab’s line, who in his sleep saw nothing but sorcery.
sentences.s326-s18: When Kavus, the cruel jailer, had no other (son), (he was) full of the color of the face and his heart full of the elixir.
sentences.s326-s19: From the East to the West (his fame) extended: greatness and kingship and crown and girdle.
sentences.s326-s20: He continually desired that he might pass beyond the sky and count the whole revolution of the stars.
sentences.s326-s37: You will regret these words; think it over and do not issue the court's decree.
sentences.s326-s38: And if you still pursue such a deed, a demon will take from you the worldly splendour, O Khedive.
sentences.s326-s39: You will remain full of pain and with a heart full of sin; henceforth they will not call you king.
sentences.s326-s40: Seek refuge in God and turn to God, for He is the guide over good and evil.
sentences.s326-s1: A grief befell the hearts of the Iranians because of the king; all stood stunned and were astray.
sentences.s326-s2: When Zal heard these words, he sprang up and drew up a cold blast from his breast.
sentences.s326-s3: He said to the Iranians that this counsel is no counsel; wisdom has no place in his brain.
sentences.s326-s4: so that I may gird my loins and stand among the worshippers before the throne of the Kians.
sentences.s326-s21: Know that I gave him many admonitions about that; I also revealed to him these same bitter words.
sentences.s326-s22: He heard many admonitions, but they did him no good; I returned from him full of burning sorrow and pain.
sentences.s326-s23: When he was overthrown, he fell into the dust; may the Pure God pardon his life.
sentences.s326-s24: He came before God having become ungrateful, with his head full of dust and his heart full of dread.
sentences.s326-s29: You carried off the Iranian women and small children in vengeance, leaving no one among them.
sentences.s326-s30: God freed you from his hand; He pardoned (him) and made your counsel firm.
sentences.s326-s31: You killed a man who was greatly afraid of him — ungrateful to the Creator and Sustainer.
sentences.s326-s32: When I said that the time was calm, there were occasions of forgiveness and shelter and cup.
sentences.s326-s5: Among kings I have seen no one who said this; when he spoke, it ought not to be hidden from us.
sentences.s326-s6: We should not be of the same mind about this — that he ever drove such a tale.
sentences.s326-s7: Except that a demon became allied with him; by the way of the divine his head was turned back.
sentences.s326-s8: Fereydun and Hushang, worshippers of God, never set hand to this work.
sentences.s326-s25: You departed, and a hundred thousand swordsmen — armored men with the mace Gâwsar.
sentences.s326-s26: Like a living lion you made the battle; you arrayed (adorned) the plain of Khwarezm.
sentences.s326-s27: From the front of the host you quickly went into battle; you became on foot, then to the battle with Pashang.
sentences.s326-s28: If he did you harm, (you) would get hold of him; you drove Afrasiab back into Iran.
sentences.s326-s13: Hear the words of the world‑wise elder; if he brings a crooked counsel, do not answer.
sentences.s326-s14: For speech, when it is true, is bitter; one should close it with bitterness and in brevity.
sentences.s326-s15: It is not fitting that you take offense at me about this truth before this assembly.
sentences.s326-s16: In the land of Turan a scion of your mother's line was born; there he is ill at ease and (is) your source of sustenance.
sentences.s326-s33: Now in Iran the situation is harder: affairs are more difficult, evils are greater, and the heart more tormented.
sentences.s326-s34: For you have turned away from the godly road: you passed into crookedness and took an evil path.
sentences.s326-s35: From this evil your body will gain no benefit; it will not be to the Creator's liking.
sentences.s326-s36: If this is so, O king, your order will not remain and no one will honor your covenant.
sentences.s326-s9: I will tell him the whole truth, if any lack should come into his inner soul.
sentences.s326-s10: Thus an answer came from the Iranians: that no one among them had spoken in this way.
sentences.s326-s11: We are all with you in what you will say to the king; may he not lose his custom and way.
sentences.s326-s12: Zāl heard these words, rose to his feet, and thus said, 'O Khosrow, [of] justice and truth...'
